title: The Black–Scholes Equation
properties: PDE: $\partial_t u + rS\,\partial_S u + \tfrac{1}{2}\Sigma^2 S^2\,\partial_S^2 u - ru = 0$, $\;u(T,S) = g(S)$.
Price = discounted risk-neutral expectation: $u(t,S) = e^{-r(T-t)}\mathbb{E}[g(S_T) \mid S_t = S]$.
Real-world drift $\mu$ absent — eliminated by replication.
Hedge: hold $\partial_S u$ shares (delta); $x = \log S$ reduces the PDE to the heat equation.
Feynman–Kac instance: geometric Brownian motion, constant potential $V = r$.
motivation: What is a fair price today for the right to buy a stock at price $K$ at a future time $T$? Black, Scholes and Merton (1973) answered by showing the option's payoff can be replicated exactly by continuously trading the stock and a bank account; no-arbitrage then forces the price to satisfy a specific parabolic PDE. Via Feynman–Kac, that PDE is equivalent to a discounted expectation — the modern 'risk-neutral pricing' formulation on which quantitative finance is built.
definition: With stock dynamics $dS_t = r S_t\,dt + \Sigma S_t\,dW_t$ under the risk-neutral measure ($r$ the interest rate, $\Sigma$ the volatility), the price $u(t,S)$ of a European claim paying $g(S_T)$ solves $$\partial_t u + r S\,\partial_S u + \tfrac{1}{2}\Sigma^2 S^2\,\partial_S^2 u - r u = 0, \qquad u(T,S) = g(S).$$ This is the Feynman–Kac PDE for geometric Brownian motion with constant potential $V = r$, so $u(t,S) = e^{-r(T-t)}\,\mathbb{E}[g(S_T) \mid S_t = S]$.
examples: Call option $g(S) = \max(S-K, 0)$: evaluating the Gaussian integral gives the closed-form Black–Scholes formula $u = S\,\Phi(d_1) - K e^{-r(T-t)}\,\Phi(d_2)$ with $d_{1,2} = \frac{\log(S/K) + (r \pm \Sigma^2/2)(T-t)}{\Sigma\sqrt{T-t}}$.
Substituting $x = \log S$ and discounting reduces the equation to the plain heat equation — the original 1973 derivation.
The drift $\mu$ of the real-world stock does not appear: replication eliminates it, which is the celebrated economic content of the model.
consequences: Risk-neutral valuation generalises far beyond this model: price = discounted expectation under a measure that makes discounted traded assets martingales.
The hedging strategy is read off the solution: hold $\partial_S u$ shares ('delta hedging') — the PDE gradient is the trading rule.
Monte Carlo pricing of high-dimensional derivatives is Feynman–Kac run numerically: simulate risk-neutral paths, average discounted payoffs.
